---
title: "PS 641 - Bivariate Regression"
author: "Robert Durbin and Prof Kuehl"
date: "`r format(Sys.time(), '%B %d, %Y')`"
editor_options: 
  chunk_output_type: console
---


Packages
```{r}
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
```

Load the traffic data(traffic.RData. Note we are using a Rproject this week so don't need to set our working directory. 
```{r}
load("traffic.rdata")
```

This dataset contains county level traffic and demographic data from every county in Illinois from the year 2019. Traffic volume comes from the Illinois Department of Transportation, crash data comes from the National Highway Transit Safety Administration, population data comes from the US Census, transit system data comes from the Champaign County Regional Planning Commission, the urban-rural data comes from the CDC.

Here's a Codebook:

| Variable | Description |
|---------|------------|
| year | The year from which data comes |
| county | The Illinois county from which data comes |
| id | A County ID variable, not important unless you are adding additional data |
| area | Total land area of a county in square miles |
| avmt | Annual Vehicle Miles Travelled is a measure of the total number of miles driven by all motor vehicles on all road types within a county-year |
| popden | Simple calculation of the population of a county divided by the total land area (should probably just make them calculate this one) |
| deaths | The total reported traffic fatalities from a county-year |
| urban | The National Center for Health Statistics' (NCHS) urban-rural classification for counties. '1' indicates a large central metro, '2' indicates a large fringe metro, '3' indicates a medium metro, '4' indicates a small metro, '5' indicates a micropolitan setting, and '6' indicates a non-core heavily rural setting |
| transit | The presence/absence of a transit system within a county. '0' indicates there is no transit system, while '1' indicates that there is a transit system |
| largetransit | Indicates a large transit system |
| smalltransit | Indicates a small transit system |
| ruralmunicipaltransit | Indicates a rural municipal transit system (I think this is smaller than "smalltransit") |
| collisions | The total reported number of traffic collisions from a county-year |
| injuries | The total reported number of injuries resulting from traffic collisions from a county-year |
| female | The reported percent of the population of a county which is female in a given county-year |
| over65 | The reported percent of the population of a county which is over the age of 65 in a given year |
| rain | The average number of days of rain a county sees in a year |
| snow | The average number of days of snow a county sees in a year |
| sun | The average number of sunny days a county sees in a year |


Explore the data: 

```{r}
table(traffic$year)

# Subset for the year 2019
traffic2019 <- subset(traffic, year == "2019")
```

Lets look at the relationship between the population of a county and its number of traffic fatalities.
```{r}
plot(traffic2019$pop, traffic2019$deaths,xlab="Population", ylab="Traffic Fatalities", main="County Population & Traffic Fatalities", pch=10) #creates a scatterplot
#What type of relationship does this look like? What terms could we use to describe it? What is the outlier and what should we do about it. 

```

We can also run this plot without Cook County as an outlier, making it easier to see the trend. 
```{r}
# Filter out rows where county is "Cook"
traffic_filtered <- traffic2019[traffic2019$county != "Cook",]

plot(traffic_filtered$pop, traffic_filtered$deaths,xlab="Population", ylab="Traffic Fatalities", main="County Population & Traffic Fatalities", pch=10) #creates a scatterplot
#What type of relationship does this look like? What terms could we use to descripte it?
``` 


```{r}
#To standardize measures let's create a per capita measure for some of our variables)

traffic2019$percapdeaths <- traffic2019$deaths/traffic2019$pop * 100000 # This command creates per capita deaths from traffic fatalities and multiplies it by 100,000 as deaths per 100,000 is the standard reporting metric for this data. 

summary(traffic2019$percapdeaths)
hist(traffic2019$percapdeaths)
hist(traffic2019$deaths)#shows importance of using per capita



traffic2019$percapavmt <- round((traffic2019$avmt/traffic2019$pop)/1000000, 9) # The "round" command...you guessed it, rounds the resulting percapavmt variable(usually we only round when reporting results). This creates a per capita measure of annual vehicle miles traveled divided by one million, again as this is the usual metric for working with this sort of data.

hist(traffic2019$percapavmt)
```


First tests of association
```{r}
cov(traffic2019$pop, traffic2019$deaths) #Covariance, in isolation can't interpret much more than sign
cor(traffic2019$pop, traffic2019$deaths) #Correlation, range from -1 to 1. Note: There is no defined rule as to when variables are significant or not. The farther from 0 the greater the relationship.
```

Can create correlation matrix with select variables. This is a good way to get a glimpse (start thinking about) the relationships between lots of variables. 
```{r}
traffic2 <- select(traffic2019, percapdeaths, percapavmt, income, popden, female)
cor(traffic2)

pairs(traffic2) #Gives a quick look graphically(numbers far more useful)
```

#Regression - woooohooooo
Lets look at the relationship between Annual Vehicle Miles Travelled (avmt) and Traffic Fatalities (deaths)

Our model: 
Per Capita Traffic Fatalities = $\beta_0$  + $\beta_1$ Per Capita Annual Vehicle Miles Travelled + e

```{r}
model1 <- lm(percapdeaths ~ percapavmt, data = traffic2019) #creates a regression model. lm = linear model. the "<-"creates an object that simplifies later steps. 

summary(model1) #What does this output tell us? ie how do we interpret the coefficients and p-values? Is the relationship statistically significant?

```

Significance P-value: 0.00203 - statistically significant at .01 level

Sign Estimate: Positive

Size Estimate: 870.564; A one unit increase in the annual vehicle miles (percapavmt) traveled in a county is associated with an additional 871 units in percapdeaths (which we have multiplied by 100,000). 

Substantive: an annual increase of one million vehicle miles traveled  is associated with 0.00871 more traffic fatalities. (alternatively) For every 115 million miles driven we expect one traffic fatality.

(The United States had more than 3.26 trillion annual vehicle miles traveled in 2022 according to the Department of Energy, so assuming the rate for Illinois in 2019 was roughly the same as it was nationwide, we would expect 28,348 traffic fatalities in 2022. We actually had 42,795.)

Now we can show it graphically(This looks familiar  . . . . .)
```{r}
plot(traffic2019$percapavmt, traffic2019$percapdeaths, xlab = "Per Capita Annual Vehicle Miles Travelled", ylab = "Per Capita Traffic Fatalities", main = "Per Capita AVMT & Traffic Fatalities", pch=10) #creates a scatterplot
abline(model1) #puts the regression line onto our plot
```

Our Model:
(Per Capita Traffic Deaths * 100,000) = $\beta_0$  + $\beta_1$ * (Per Capita AVMT/1,000,000) + e
(Per Capita Traffic Deaths * 100,000) = 6.088 + 870.564 * (Per Capita AVMT/1,000,000) + e


Break: Is this causal? 


Do it yourself
Find a relationship you are interested in testing(use the codebook). Raise your hand when you're ready to try to interpret it for the the class.(Yes, I'm serious)
```{r}

```



Can add a confidence interval. It is often not necessary, but it helps us visualize the uncertainty of our estimates. 
```{r}
plot((traffic2019$percapavmt*1000000), traffic2019$percapdeaths, xlab = "Per Capita Annual Vehicle Miles Travelled", ylab = "Per Capita Traffic Fatalities", main = "Per Capita AVMT & Traffic Fatalities", sub="95% Confidence Interval")  # To make this part of the analysis easier, we multiply the x axis by 1,000,000
abline(model2, pch=10, col="blue") 


newx <- seq(5000, 35000, 1000) # creates sequence between .2 and .5 by .01 (Use your x-axis range for this)
a <- predict(model2, newdata = data.frame(otherpercapavmt = newx), interval = "confidence") #predicts values for sequence
lines(newx,a[,2], lty=5, col = "red") #add lines
lines(newx,a[,3], lty=5, col = "red")
#This shows the 95% confidence interval. 

#### We can also see what a 99% confidence interval looks like. 
b <- predict(model2, newdata=data.frame(otherpercapavmt=newx), interval="confidence", level=.99)
lines(newx,b[,2], lty=6, col="green")
lines(newx,b[,3], lty=6, col="green")

#Why does the 99% have a larger confidence interval
```

We can then make predictions based on certain counties' per capita AVMT:
```{r}
plot((traffic2019$percapavmt*1000000), traffic2019$percapdeaths, xlab = "Per Capita Annual Vehicle Miles Travelled", ylab = "Per Capita Traffic Fatalities", main = "Per Capita AVMT & Traffic Fatalities") 
abline(model2, col="red", lwd = 2)
abline(v=8063.393, col="blue", lwd = 2) # Show prediction for Counties with Per Capita AVMT of 8063.393 (Number picked for no particular reason)

model2$fitted.values[which(traffic2019$percapavmt==0.008063393)] # Extract predictions 
mean(model2$fitted.values[which(traffic2019$percapavmt==0.008063393)]) # Extract mean prediction 
abline(h = 13.10732, col="blue", lwd=2) #show mean

#can combine if we're feeling fancy
abline(h=(mean(model2$fitted.values[which(traffic2019$percapavmt==0.008063393)])), col="green", lwd=2, lty=3)


#or if not just plug it into our equation
6.088 + 870.564*0.008063393
```



#### Your next challenge
Rerun your analysis for a different year(don't forget about find and replace). How do the results change?
*bonus if you're bored: add county label to each point
```{r}









```



Other regression commands you may find useful:
```{r}
coef(model1) #coefficients
confint(model1) #confidence intervals
resid(model1) #residuals
deviance(model1) #residual sum of squares
```



More Bonus code: GGplot versions of above

```{r}
p <- ggplot(traffic2019, aes(x = percapavmt, y = percapdeaths))+   #using ggplot and dataset
 geom_point(shape=10) + #basic scatter plot
  geom_smooth(method = "lm")+
  labs(title="Per Capita AVMT & Traffic Fatalities")+ xlab("Per Capita AVMT")+ ylab("Per Capita Traffic Fatalities")
p

p + theme_classic()

#install.packages("ggthemes") #https://yutannihilation.github.io/allYourFigureAreBelongToUs/ggthemes/
library("ggthemes")
p + theme_economist()
 

#Show County Names
ggplot(traffic2019, aes(x = (percapavmt*1000000), y = percapdeaths, label = county))+   #using ggplot and dataset
 geom_text() + # Change the geom type
  geom_smooth(method = "lm")+
  labs(title="Per Capita AVMT & Traffic Fatalities") + xlab("Per Capita AVMT") + ylab("Per Capita Traffic Fatalities")

#Messy!!!


#Just label dekalb
traffic2019$dekalb <- ifelse(traffic2019$county=="DeKalb", "DeKalb", "")  # Pick county you want to have labeled & specify how you want it labeled)
table(traffic2019$dekalb)  

w <- ggplot(traffic2019, aes(x = (percapavmt*1000000), y = percapdeaths, label = dekalb))+  
  geom_point(shape = 10) +
  geom_text(hjust = -.001, nudge_x = 0.0005, color = "red") + 
  geom_smooth(method = "lm")+
  labs(title="Per Capita AVMT & Traffic Fatalities") + xlab("Per Capita AVMT") + ylab("Per Capita Traffic Fatalities")

w
  
# We're at the bottom left. What does it mean that we're below the regression curve? 
```
